title: SOURCE NEEMISHA BANSAL 120 GRAMMAR RULES TOPIC ERROR QU

questions.question: [RULE 31: "SO...AS" VS "AS...AS"]
options: (a) He is (b) as intelligent (c) so his (d) elder brother.
questions.explanation: Positive comparison mein 'as...as' ka pair banta hai. 'So' ki jagah 'as' ka use hona chahiye. 'As intelligent as' is the correct pair for comparison.

questions.question: Q202. (a) She is (b) not so (c) tall as (d) her mother.
questions.explanation: Negative sentences mein 'so...as' ka use bilkul sahi hai. Negative comparisons can use both 'so...as' and 'as...as'.

questions.question: Q203. (a) The Ganga is (b) so sacred (c) as any other (d) river in India.
questions.explanation: Affirmative (positive) sentence mein 'so...as' ka use nahi hota, sirf 'as...as' ka hota hai. 'So...as' is reserved for negative sentences.

questions.question: Q204. (a) This task (b) is not (c) as difficult (d) No Error.
questions.explanation: Negative sentence mein 'as...as' ka prayog bhi sahi maana jata hai. It's a valid structure in both positive and negative contexts.

questions.question: Q205. (a) He ran (b) so fast (c) as he (d) could.
questions.explanation: Yahan comparison positive sense mein hai, isliye 'as fast as' sahi hoga. 'So...as' is typically for negative intensity or comparisons.

questions.question: [RULE 32: "MANY" (COUNTABLE) VS "MUCH" (UNCOUNTABLE)]
options: (a) There is (b) many sugar (c) in the (d) container.
questions.explanation: 'Sugar' uncountable noun hai, isliye 'many' ki jagah 'much' ka use hoga. 'Much' is used with uncountable nouns like sugar, water, and sand.

questions.question: Q207. (a) I don't (b) have (c) much (d) friends.
questions.explanation: 'Friends' countable plural noun hai, isliye 'many' ka use hona chahiye. 'Many' is used with plural countable nouns.

questions.question: Q208. (a) How (b) much books (c) do you (d) need for the exam?
questions.explanation: 'Books' count kiye ja sakte hain, isliye 'much' galat hai, 'many' sahi hai. Countable nouns take 'many'.

questions.question: Q209. (a) She has (b) much work (c) to finish (d) No Error.
questions.explanation: 'Work' uncountable hai, isliye 'much' ka prayog bilkul sahi hai. Uncountable nouns take 'much'.

questions.question: Q210. (a) Many a (b) wisdom (c) is gained (d) through experience.
questions.explanation: 'Wisdom' uncountable (abstract) noun hai, iske saath 'Many a' (jo ki countable ke liye hai) nahi, balki 'Much' ya 'A lot of' aayega.

questions.question: [RULE 33: "WHO" VS "WHOM"]
options: (a) The person (b) who I (c) met yesterday (d) was very kind.
questions.explanation: Yahan hum object ki baat kar rahe hain (met whom?), isliye 'whom' aayega. 'Whom' is the objective case used when the person is the receiver of the action.

questions.question: Q212. (a) Who do (b) you think (c) will win (d) the match?
questions.explanation: Yahan 'who' subject hai 'will win' verb ke liye, isliye sentence sahi hai. 'Who' acts as the subject of the following verb.

questions.question: Q213. (a) This is (b) the man (c) whom (d) saved my life.
questions.explanation: 'Saved' verb ke liye subject chahiye, isliye 'whom' ki jagah 'who' aayega. Always use 'who' when it is the doer of the verb.

questions.question: Q214. (a) Whomever (b) is responsible (c) should be (d) punished.
questions.explanation: 'Is' verb ka subject 'Whoever' hona chahiye, 'Whomever' nahi. Subjective pronoun is required for the verb 'is'.

questions.question: Q215. (a) The girl (b) whom you (c) saw is (d) my sister.
questions.explanation: 'You saw (her)' - 'her' object hai, isliye 'whom' bilkul sahi hai. 'Whom' correctly identifies the object of the verb 'saw'.

questions.question: [RULE 34: ADVERBS WITH "VERBS OF SENSATION"]
options: (a) The flower (b) smells (c) sweetly (d) in the garden.
questions.explanation: 'Smell' ek verb of sensation hai, iske saath adverb (sweetly) nahi, adjective (sweet) aayega. Verbs of sensation (taste, feel, smell) take adjectives.

questions.question: Q217. (a) He looks (b) sadly (c) because he (d) lost his job.
questions.explanation: 'Look' sensation/state dikhata hai, isliye adjective 'sad' aayega. Adverbs describe 'how' an action is done, but here we describe the subject's state.

questions.question: Q218. (a) This pizza (b) tastes (c) delicious (d) No Error.
questions.explanation: 'Delicious' ek adjective hai jo 'taste' verb ke saath sahi baithta hai. Sensation verbs are followed by adjectives, not adverbs.

questions.question: Q219. (a) She feels (b) badly (c) about the (d) incident.
questions.explanation: 'Feel' ke saath adjective 'bad' aana chahiye. 'Badly' tab use hota hai jab touch karne ki ability kharab ho. Standard grammar uses 'feel bad' for emotions.

questions.question: Q220. (a) The music (b) sounds (c) loudly (d) to my ears.
questions.explanation: 'Sound' sensation hai, isliye adjective 'loud' ka prayog hoga. Adverbs are not used with verbs that link the subject to a quality.

questions.question: [RULE 35: "LITTLE" / "A LITTLE" / "THE LITTLE"]
options: (a) There is (b) little hope (c) of his (d) recovery (meaning: no hope).
questions.explanation: 'Little' ka matlab hota hai 'na ke barabar' (negative), jo sentence ke context mein sahi hai. 'Little' means almost none.

questions.question: Q222. (a) Please give (b) me (c) little water (d) to drink.
questions.explanation: Pine ke liye thoda paani chahiye, isliye 'a little' (positive sense) aayega. 'Little' means nothing, while 'a little' means some amount.

questions.question: Q223. (a) I spent (b) the little (c) money I (d) had (No Error).
questions.explanation: 'The little' ka matlab hai 'jitna bhi thoda bahut tha sab'. Ye sahi use hai. 'The little' refers to the whole of a small quantity.

questions.question: Q224. (a) A little (b) knowledge is (c) a dangerous (d) thing.
questions.explanation: Ye ek mashhoor kahawat hai, jisme 'a little' (thoda sa) bilkul sahi hai. It refers to having some but incomplete knowledge.

questions.question: Q225. (a) He has (b) a little (c) sense, so he (d) failed (meaning: no sense).
questions.explanation: Agar wo fail ho gaya, matlab usme 'na ke barabar' akal thi. Isliye sirf 'little' (negative) aayega. 'Little' denotes a lack of something.

questions.question: [RULE 36: "FEW" / "A FEW" / "THE FEW"]
options: (a) Few (b) students can (c) solve this (d) difficult sum (meaning: very few).
questions.explanation: 'Few' countable ke liye negative sense (na ke barabar) mein sahi hai. 'Few' is used for a negligible number of countable items.

questions.question: Q227. (a) I have (b) a few (c) friends in (d) the city (positive sense).
questions.explanation: 'A few' ka matlab thode dost (positive), jo gramatically correct hai. 'A few' means some, though not many.

questions.question: Q228. (a) The few (b) clothes he (c) had were (d) all torn.
questions.explanation: 'The few' ka matlab hai 'jo bhi thode bahut the', isliye ye sahi hai. It refers to the specific small number that exists.

questions.question: Q229. (a) Few (b) days that (c) I spent (d) with her were happy.
questions.explanation: Jab aage explanation di ho ('that I spent'), toh 'The few' ka use hota hai. 'The few' is used when defining the small number mentioned.

questions.question: Q230. (a) He is (b) lonely because (c) he has (d) a few friends.
questions.explanation: Agar wo lonely hai, toh uske dost 'na ke barabar' honge. 'A few' ki jagah sirf 'few' aayega. Lonely people have 'few' (hardly any) friends.

questions.question: [RULE 37: "ELDER" VS "OLDER"]
options: (a) He is (b) elder than (c) his friend (d) by two years.
questions.explanation: 'Elder' ke saath 'than' nahi lagta, aur ye sirf family members ke liye hota hai. Yahan 'older than' sahi hai. 'Elder' is used for blood relations and doesn't take 'than'.

questions.question: Q232. (a) My (b) older brother (c) is a (d) doctor.
questions.explanation: Sagi bhai (family) ke liye 'elder' ka use behtar hota hai. 'Elder' is preferred for seniority within a family.

questions.question: Q233. (a) She is (b) the eldest (c) of the (d) four sisters.
questions.explanation: Family mein sabse bade ke liye 'eldest' bilkul sahi hai. 'Eldest' is the superlative form for family seniority.

questions.question: Q234. (a) This temple (b) is (c) elder than (d) that one.
questions.explanation: Non-living things ya janwaro ke liye 'elder' nahi 'older' use hota hai. 'Elder' is strictly for humans (usually family).

questions.question: Q235. (a) Ram is (b) elder to (c) his (d) sister.
questions.explanation: 'Elder' ke saath preposition 'to' lagta hai, jo yahan sahi hai. While 'Older' takes 'than', 'Elder' takes 'to'.

questions.question: [RULE 38: "STILL" VS "YET"]
options: (a) He has (b) still not (c) finished (d) the work.
questions.explanation: Negative sentences mein progress dikhane ke liye 'yet' ka use end mein hota hai: 'He hasn't finished yet'. 'Still' typically goes before the verb.

questions.question: Q237. (a) Is it (b) still (c) raining (d) outside?
questions.explanation: Continue hone wale kaam ke liye 'still' sahi hai. 'Still' refers to an action that is ongoing.

questions.question: Q238. (a) The train (b) hasn't (c) arrived (d) still.
questions.explanation: Sentence ke end mein negative context mein 'yet' aayega, 'still' nahi. 'Yet' is used at the end of negative sentences or questions.

questions.question: Q239. (a) I (b) yet (c) remember (d) that day.
questions.explanation: Affirmative sentence mein action jari hone par 'still' lagta hai: 'I still remember'. 'Yet' is not used in simple affirmative statements like this.

questions.question: Q240. (a) Have you (b) finished (c) your dinner (d) yet?
questions.explanation: Questions mein 'yet' ka use bilkul sahi hai. 'Yet' asks if an expected action has occurred.

questions.question: [RULE 39: "TOO MUCH" VS "MUCH TOO"]
options: (a) It is (b) too much (c) painful (d) for me.
questions.explanation: 'Painful' ek adjective hai, isliye iske pehle 'much too' aayega. 'Much too' is followed by an adjective.

questions.question: Q242. (a) His (b) behavior gave (c) me (d) much too pain.
questions.explanation: 'Pain' ek noun hai, isliye iske pehle 'too much' aayega. 'Too much' is followed by a noun.

questions.question: Q243. (a) There is (b) too much (c) noise (d) in the class.
questions.explanation: 'Noise' noun hai, isliye 'too much' ka use sahi hai. 'Too much' correctly modifies the noun 'noise'.

questions.question: Q244. (a) The tea (b) is (c) much too (d) hot.
questions.explanation: 'Hot' adjective hai, isliye 'much too' sahi hai. 'Much too' is the correct intensifier for adjectives.

questions.question: Q245. (a) He is (b) much too (c) jealousy (d) of his brother.
questions.explanation: 'Much too' ke baad adjective aana chahiye, 'jealousy' noun hai. 'Jealous' (adjective) ka use karein. Use the adjective form after 'much too'.

questions.question: [RULE 40: "HARD" VS "HARDLY"]
options: (a) He (b) works (c) hardly (d) to support his family.
questions.explanation: 'Hardly' ka matlab hota hai 'na ke barabar'. Mehnat karne ke liye 'works hard' sahi hai. 'Hard' is the adverb of effort; 'hardly' means almost not.

questions.question: Q247. (a) I can (b) hard (c) believe (d) what he said.
questions.explanation: 'Mushkil se' ke sense mein 'hardly' aayega. 'Hardly' is used to express difficulty or rarity of an action.

questions.question: Q248. (a) It is (b) a (c) hard (d) nut to crack.
questions.explanation: 'Hard' yahan adjective ki tarah use hua hai, jo ki correct hai. 'Hard' describes the difficulty or solid state of something.

questions.question: Q249. (a) She (b) hardly (c) ever (d) goes out.
questions.explanation: 'Hardly ever' ka matlab hai 'shayad hi kabhi', jo ki bilkul sahi use hai. This is a common and correct adverbial phrase.

questions.question: Q250. (a) He (b) hit the (c) ball (d) hardly.
questions.explanation: Zor se maarne ke liye 'hit the ball hard' sahi hota hai. 'Hardly' yahan meaning change kar deta hai (mushkil se mara). Use 'hard' for physical force.
